parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: I noticed my chest gets tight whenever I hear a sudden loud noise, which is weird because it never used to bother me at all. I just sit there waiting for the next thing to happen, feeling completely on edge for no real reason. My hands start sweating and I have to consciously remind myself to breathe normally. It feels like my body is stuck in this weird alert mode over absolutely nothing. I try to shake it off, but the tension just lingers in my shoulders all day.
turns.model_response:
That sounds really uncomfortable, and also pretty exhausting to carry around all day. What you’re describing—tight chest, sweating, feeling “on edge,” holding your breath, lingering shoulder tension—can happen when your body gets stuck in a heightened stress response, even if your mind isn’t seeing an obvious reason for it.

A few things that may help in the moment:

- **Slow the exhale**: Try inhaling gently for 4, then exhaling for 6–8. A longer exhale can help signal “safe” to your nervous system.
- **Ground yourself quickly**: Look around and name 5 things you can see, 4 you can feel, 3 you can hear. This can interrupt the alarm feeling.
- **Unclench the body on purpose**: Drop your shoulders, unclench your jaw, loosen your hands. Sometimes the body stays braced until you deliberately release it.
- **Reduce the “waiting for danger” loop**: If you notice yourself bracing for the next noise, try telling yourself, “My body is reacting, but I’m not in immediate danger.”

It may also be worth paying attention to **when this started**—for example, after a stressful period, poor sleep, caffeine, illness, or a scary event can make your system more jumpy than usual. If this is happening often, getting worse, or affecting your daily life, it could be really worthwhile to talk with a doctor or mental health professional, just to rule out physical causes and get support.

And if the chest tightness ever feels severe, happens with shortness of breath, fainting, pain spreading to your arm/jaw, or feels different than usual, it’s important to get urgent medical help.

If you want, I can help you sort through whether this seems more like stress/anxiety, a startle response, or something physical—and we can think through what’s been going on lately.

turns.user_message: This constant state of readiness has completely taken over my routine, and I keep wondering why my nerves are so frayed all the time now. I catch myself jumping at the smallest sounds, like a door closing down the hall or a phone vibrating on the table. My stomach knots up before I even know what triggered it, leaving me exhausted from just trying to stay calm. It used to be so easy to brush things off, but now every little shift in the room feels like a warning sign. I am just so tired of feeling like I am waiting for the other shoe to drop.
turns.model_response:
That sounds really exhausting. Living like your body is always braced for something bad can wear you down fast, especially when even small noises or changes start feeling like threats. Nothing about that sounds “small” to me — it sounds like you’ve been carrying a lot of tension for a while.

You don’t need to force yourself to dismiss it, but it may help to treat this as a real stress response, not a personal failing. A few things that can sometimes help in the moment:

- **Name what’s happening:** “My body is on alert right now.”
- **Slow the exhale:** try breathing out longer than you breathe in, for a minute or two.
- **Orient to the room:** look for 5 things you can see, 4 you can feel, 3 you can hear.
- **Reduce input if you can:** step away from noise, bright screens, or anything that keeps your system revved up.
- **Check the basics:** sleep, caffeine, hunger, and overstimulation can all make this worse.

If this has been going on a lot, it could be worth talking to a therapist or a doctor — not because anything is “wrong” with you, but because persistent hyper-alertness is something you deserve support with. If you want, I can also help you figure out what might be contributing to it or talk through ways to feel a little safer in the moment.

turns.user_message: I feel completely trapped inside my own skin because my body refuses to stand down even when everything around me is perfectly quiet. Every single breath feels heavy and shallow, like my lungs are fighting against an invisible weight that just will not lift. I pace back and forth trying to burn off this frantic energy, but my heart keeps hammering against my ribs like a trapped bird. It is exhausting to live like this when I know there is absolutely nothing to be afraid of right now. I just want my nervous system to finally remember how to relax and let me be still.
turns.model_response:
That sounds incredibly exhausting and scary. I’m sorry you’re stuck in that “my body won’t calm down” state — it can feel so unfair when your mind knows you’re safe but your body is still sounding the alarm.

A few things you can try right now, just to give your nervous system a different signal:

- **Longer exhale than inhale:** Try inhaling for 4, exhaling for 6–8, for a minute or two. Don’t force deep breaths; just make the exhale slow.
- **Grounding through the senses:** Name **5 things you can see, 4 you can feel, 3 you can hear, 2 you can smell, 1 you can taste.**
- **Press into support:** Put both feet on the floor, or press your palms together firmly for 10 seconds and release.
- **Cold sensation:** Splash cool water on your face or hold something cold for a moment if that feels okay.
- **Tiny movement, then stillness:** A short walk, shoulder rolls, or stretching can help burn off adrenaline — then try sitting with a blanket or pillow for support.

If it helps, you can also remind yourself: **“This is my body being activated, not proof that I’m in danger. It will pass.”**

If this is happening often, getting worse, or affecting your ability to breathe, sleep, or function, it would be a good idea to talk to a medical professional or therapist. And if you ever feel like you might hurt yourself or you can’t stay safe, call emergency services or a crisis line right away.

If you want, I can stay with you and walk you through a 60-second calming exercise right now.
